text: The surgeon performs a unilateral opening of the endopelvic fascia on the left side, leaving the right side closed to maintain pelvic floor tension. The anterior base of the prostate is defined by the curvature visible from the open endopelvic fascia. The anterior bladder neck dissection continues, followed by a modified nerve-sparing technique due to the closed endopelvic fascia. Hemlock clips are applied to release the dorsolateral vascular pedicle at the base. The fourth arm rotates the prostate out of the pelvis, aiding in the nerve-sparing procedure, which is performed in an antegrade fashion. A retrograde approach is also demonstrated, with hemlock clips bridging the gap on the dorsolateral pedicle.

The surgeon corrects the dissection from the interfacial to the intrafacial plane, peeling off a closer layer before proceeding to the anterior fibromuscular tissue. The anterior apron is released from the base of the prostate using monopolar diathermy, maintaining distance from the neurovascular bundle. The prostate is retracted dorsally and cranially, and heat is applied to hug the anterior fibromuscular surface, minimizing the opening of DVC vessels. Cold cutting with scissors is also used to advance under the DVC with minimal vessel opening, aided by a clean ventral lateral dissection on the right side.

The surgeon proceeds towards the urethra at the apex of the prostate, aiming to preserve maximal urethral length. The urethra is sharply divided, revealing the verumontanum within the prosthetic urethra. The prostate is then removed and placed in an endocatch bag, leaving a prostatic bed that appears shell-like. The DVC is visible but does not require oversewing.
